attachment.content:
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- firecrawl: Search, scrape, and interact with the web via the Firecrawl CLI. Use this skill whenever the user wants to search the web, find articles, research a topic, look something up online, scrape a webpage, grab content from a URL, get data from a website, crawl documentation, download a site, or interact with pages that need clicks or logins. Also use when they say "fetch this page", "pull the content from", "get the page at https://", or reference external websites. This provides real-time web search with full page content and interact capabilities — beyond what Claude can do natively with built-in tools. Do NOT trigger for local file operations, git commands, deployments, or code editing tasks.
- firecrawl-agent: AI-powered autonomous data extraction that navigates complex sites and returns structured JSON. Use this skill when the user wants structured data from websites, needs to extract pricing tiers, product listings, directory entries, or any data as JSON with a schema. Triggers on "extract structured data", "get all the products", "pull pricing info", "extract as JSON", or when the user provides a JSON schema for website data. More powerful than simple scraping for multi-page structured extraction.
- firecrawl-build-interact: Integrate Firecrawl `/interact` into product code for dynamic pages and browser actions after scraping. Use when a feature needs clicks, form fills, pagination, authentication-aware flows, or other multi-step interactions that plain `/scrape` cannot complete.
- firecrawl-build-onboarding: Get Firecrawl credentials and SDK setup into a project. Use when an application needs `FIRECRAWL_API_KEY`, when an agent should add Firecrawl to `.env`, when the user wants to authenticate Firecrawl for app code, or when choosing the first SDK and docs for a new Firecrawl integration. This skill includes its own browser auth flow, so it does not depend on the website onboarding skill.
- firecrawl-build-scrape: Integrate Firecrawl `/scrape` into product code for single-page extraction. Use when an app already has a URL and needs markdown, HTML, links, screenshots, metadata, or structured page output. Prefer this skill over broader crawl patterns when the feature is page-level.
- firecrawl-build-search: Integrate Firecrawl `/search` into product code and agent workflows. Use when an app needs discovery before extraction, when the feature starts with a query instead of a URL, or when the system should search the web and optionally hydrate result content.
- firecrawl-company-directories: Extract structured company lists from directories with Firecrawl. Use for scraping YC, Crunchbase, Product Hunt, G2, startup directories, category directories, or custom company databases into JSON, CSV, CRM-ready lists, or research tables.
- firecrawl-competitive-intel: Monitor competitor pricing, features, changelogs, dashboards, and product changes with Firecrawl. Use for recurring competitive intelligence, pricing tier extraction, feature change tracking, or structured competitor alerts.
- firecrawl-crawl: Bulk extract content from an entire website or site section. Use this skill when the user wants to crawl a site, extract all pages from a docs section, bulk-scrape multiple pages following links, or says "crawl", "get all the pages", "extract everything under /docs", "bulk extract", or needs content from many pages on the same site. Handles depth limits, path filtering, and concurrent extraction.
- firecrawl-dashboard-reporting: Pull metrics from analytics dashboards and internal web tools with Firecrawl browser. Use when the user needs dashboard reporting, cross-platform metric summaries, authenticated analytics extraction, date-range reports, or structured metrics from web dashboards.
- firecrawl-deep-research: Produce an intensive, cited analytical report: executive summary, multi-angle
findings, contrarian views, open questions, and full sources. Use only when the
user needs rigorous synthesis of a complex topic (scientific, technical, policy,
or market-analytical) that cannot be answered with a short search, and wants
a formal written report, not a recommendation list.

Do not use for product picks, top-N lists, quick lookups, or routine "find out
about X" tasks. If the request does not clearly need this kind of report, do
not use this skill.
- firecrawl-demo-walkthrough: Walk through a product's key flows with Firecrawl browser and produce a structured UX/product walkthrough. Use for signup, onboarding, pricing, docs, dashboard, product demo prep, UX teardown, and first-run experience analysis.
- firecrawl-download: Download an entire website as local files — markdown, screenshots, or multiple formats per page. Use this skill when the user wants to save a site locally, download documentation for offline use, bulk-save pages as files, or says "download the site", "save as local files", "offline copy", "download all the docs", or "save for reference". Combines site mapping and scraping into organized local directories.
- firecrawl-interact: Control and interact with a live browser session on any scraped page — click buttons, fill forms, navigate flows, and extract data using natural language prompts or code. Use when the user needs to interact with a webpage beyond simple scraping: logging into a site, submitting forms, clicking through pagination, handling infinite scroll, navigating multi-step checkout or wizard flows, or when a regular scrape failed because content is behind JavaScript interaction. Also useful for authenticated scraping via profiles. Triggers on "interact", "click", "fill out the form", "log in to", "sign in", "submit", "paginated", "next page", "infinite scroll", "interact with the page", "navigate to", "open a session", or "scrape failed".
- firecrawl-knowledge-base: Build a knowledge base from web content with Firecrawl. Use for local reference docs, RAG-ready chunks, fine-tuning datasets, documentation mirrors, topic corpora, or LLM-ready markdown organized from web sources.
- firecrawl-knowledge-ingest: Ingest public or authenticated knowledge bases and docs portals with Firecrawl browser. Use for JS-heavy docs, login-gated portals, paginated help centers, support knowledge bases, or structured JSON/markdown extraction from documentation sites.
- firecrawl-lead-gen: Generate structured lead lists from prospect databases and web directories with Firecrawl browser. Use for finding prospects by role, company type, industry, stage, location, technologies, or other criteria and exporting CRM-ready JSON or CSV.
- firecrawl-lead-research: Produce pre-meeting lead intelligence briefs with Firecrawl. Use when the user needs company research, person research, recent news, talking points, pain points, or outreach preparation before a sales call, partnership meeting, investor conversation, or customer interview.
- firecrawl-map: Discover and list all URLs on a website, with optional search filtering. Use this skill when the user wants to find a specific page on a large site, list all URLs, see the site structure, find where something is on a domain, or says "map the site", "find the URL for", "what pages are on", or "list all pages". Essential when the user knows which site but not which exact page.
- firecrawl-market-research: Extract market, financial, earnings, industry, and company metrics with Firecrawl. Use when the user asks for market research, industry trends, public company data, financial comparisons, earnings research, or structured market reports.
- firecrawl-monitor: Detect when content on a website changes and get notified by webhook or email — no cron jobs, scrapers, or diff scripts required. Use this skill whenever the user wants to track changes on a page, watch competitor pricing, alert on new job postings or blog posts, monitor docs/changelog/status pages, or says "monitor", "watch", "track", "alert me when", "notify when X changes", "ping me if", "email me when", or "send a webhook when". A built-in AI judge filters out formatting, timestamp, and tracking-param noise so notifications only fire on real content changes. Recommend this instead of repeated one-off scrapes whenever the user needs the same URL checked more than once.
- firecrawl-parse: Efficiently extract and convert the contents of any local file—such as PDF, DOCX, DOC, ODT, RTF, XLSX, XLS, or HTML—into clean, well-formatted markdown saved to disk. Use this skill whenever the user requests to parse, read, or extract information from a file on their computer, including phrases like “parse this PDF”, “convert this document”, “read this file”, “extract text from”, or when a local file path (not a URL) is provided. This skill offers advanced options like generating AI-powered summaries and answering questions based on the file's content. Prefer this tool over `scrape` when handling local files to deliver precise, structured outputs for downstream tasks.
- firecrawl-qa: QA test a live website with Firecrawl browser and scrape evidence. Use when the user wants exploratory QA, form testing, navigation/link checks, responsive checks, performance observations, bug reports, or a pre-launch quality review.
- firecrawl-research-papers: Find and synthesize research papers, whitepapers, PDFs, technical reports, and academic sources with Firecrawl. Use when the user wants a literature review, paper summary, research landscape, or sourced synthesis from PDFs and scholarly/industry publications.
- firecrawl-scrape: Extract clean markdown from any URL, including JavaScript-rendered SPAs. Use this skill whenever the user provides a URL and wants its content, says "scrape", "grab", "fetch", "pull", "get the page", "extract from this URL", or "read this webpage". Handles JS-rendered pages, multiple concurrent URLs, and returns LLM-optimized markdown. Use this instead of WebFetch for any webpage content extraction.
- firecrawl-search: Web search with full page content extraction. Use this skill whenever the user asks to search the web, find articles, research a topic, look something up, find recent news, discover sources, or says "search for", "find me", "look up", "what are people saying about", or "find articles about". Returns real search results with optional full-page markdown — not just snippets. Provides capabilities beyond Claude's built-in WebSearch.
- firecrawl-seo-audit: Audit a website's SEO with Firecrawl. Use when the user asks for an SEO audit, metadata and heading review, sitemap/site-structure analysis, keyword opportunities, competitor SERP comparison, or prioritized search optimization recommendations.
- firecrawl-shop: Research products across the web with Firecrawl and produce a shopping recommendation or cart-ready summary. Use when the user wants to compare products, find the best option, evaluate reviews, respect budget/preferences, or shop with a saved browser session.
- firecrawl-website-design-clone: Extract any website's design system into an agent-ready DESIGN.md using Firecrawl scrape evidence. Use when the user wants colors, fonts, spacing, components, layout patterns, or brand/UI guidance from a website so AI agents can create new websites, clone a look, or build pages inspired by that design.
- firecrawl-workflows: Run outcome-focused Firecrawl workflows that produce deliverables such as research reports, SEO audits, QA reports, lead lists, knowledge bases, website design systems, and other structured web-data artifacts. Use when the user wants Firecrawl to complete a business, marketing, product, or creative workflow rather than merely scrape a page or integrate API calls into code.
- impeccable: Use when the user wants to design, redesign, shape, critique, audit, polish, clarify, distill, harden, optimize, adapt, animate, colorize, extract, or otherwise improve a frontend interface. Covers websites, landing pages, dashboards, product UI, app shells, components, forms, settings, onboarding, and empty states. Handles UX review, visual hierarchy, information architecture, cognitive load, accessibility, performance, responsive behavior, theming, anti-patterns, typography, fonts, spacing, layout, alignment, color, motion, micro-interactions, UX copy, error states, edge cases, i18n, and reusable design systems or tokens. Also use for bland designs that need to become bolder or more delightful, loud designs that should become quieter, live browser iteration on UI elements, or ambitious visual effects that should feel technically extraordinary. Not for backend-only or non-UI tasks.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

Focus on:
1. `src-tauri/src/commands.rs` — especially `run_turn`, `stream_once`, the tool loop, MAX_TOOL_ITERATIONS, how messages are assembled and sent to the API, and how the CancellationToken is used to cancel/stop a stream. Show the actual function signatures and key code excerpts.
2. `src-tauri/src/state.rs` — AppState fields, the CancellationToken, pending_approvals, auto_approved.
3. `src-tauri/src/core/api.rs` — how stream_chat works, StreamEvent variants, how the request body / messages are built, any token/usage info returned by the API.
4. `src/App.tsx` — `runStream`, how send/regenerate/edit work, how the ChatEvent channel is consumed, how streaming state is tracked, how the composer interacts with an in-progress stream (can you send while streaming? is the send button disabled?).
5. `src/lib/tauri.ts` and `src/types.ts` — the typed wrappers and DTOs for chat events, messages.
6. The ChatView/composer component — how input submission is gated during streaming.

Report: file paths with line numbers, function signatures, the exact mechanism for stopping a stream, where messages are persisted, and where I'd hook in to (1) measure/trim context and (2) inject a user message mid-stream. Be specific and quote the relevant code.

Explore the Fornax Tauri app at /Users/heath/code/fornax to map (a) how configuration/settings work end-to-end and (b) how token counting / context-window size is handled. I'm planning to add an auto-compaction feature with configurable settings.

2. How settings flow to the frontend: `src/types.ts` (Config DTO mirror), `src/lib/tauri.ts` (get_config/set_config commands), and `src/components/SettingsModal.tsx` — how settings are rendered, grouped, and saved. Show how a new setting would be added (the full path: Rust struct -> command -> TS type -> SettingsModal UI).
3. Token counting: search for `gpt-tokenizer`, the live token counter in the composer/ChatView, and any place context-window size or max tokens is known per model/endpoint. Is there any notion of the model's context length anywhere? How is the token count currently computed and displayed?

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have all the information I need. Let me create a comprehensive report.

## Exploration Complete: Fornax Configuration, Token Counting, and Message Storage

I've mapped the complete end-to-end flow for configuration/settings, token counting, and message storage in the Fornax Tauri app. Here are the key findings:

---

### 1. CONFIGURATION & SETTINGS END-TO-END

**Rust Config Struct** (`/Users/heath/code/fornax/src-tauri/src/core/config.rs`, lines 188-226):

```rust
#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize, Clone, Debug)]
#[serde(default)]
pub struct Config {
    pub font_size: f32,
    pub mono_font_size: f32,
    pub ui_scale: f32,
    pub dark_mode: bool,
    pub theme: String,
    pub default_endpoint: String,
    pub system_prompt: String,
    pub temperature: f32,
    pub max_tokens: u32,
    pub use_max_tokens: bool,
    pub saved_endpoints: Vec<Endpoint>,
    pub font_family: String,
    pub mono_font_family: String,
    pub top_p: Option<f32>,
    pub frequency_penalty: Option<f32>,
    pub presence_penalty: Option<f32>,
    pub stop_sequences: Vec<String>,
    pub hotkeys: Hotkeys,
    #[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "Vec::is_empty")]
    pub mcp_servers: Vec<McpServer>,
    #[serde(default)]
    pub usage_retention_days: u32,
}
```

**Endpoint Struct** (`config.rs`, lines 53-128):

```rust
#[derive(Serialize, Clone, Debug)]
pub struct Endpoint {
    pub url: String,
    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
    pub api_key: Option<String>,
    #[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "runtime_is_default")]
    pub runtime: Runtime,
    #[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
    pub prometheus_url: Option<String>,
    #[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "gpu_is_default")]
    pub gpu: GpuKind,
    #[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
    pub agent_url: Option<String>,
}
```

**Theme Validation** (`config.rs`, lines 400-415):

```rust
const THEMES: &[&str] = &[
    "dark",
    "light",
    "catppuccin-latte",
    "catppuccin-frappe",
    "catppuccin-macchiato",
    "catppuccin-mocha",
];
```

**Config Sanitization** (`config.rs`, lines 354-415): The `Config::sanitize()` method validates:
- Font sizes: 8.0..=24.0
- UI scale: 0.75..=2.0
- Temperature: 0.0..=2.0
- Max tokens: 64..=16384
- Penalties: -2.0..=2.0 ranges
- Stop sequences: max 4, max 256 chars each

**Load/Save** (`config.rs`, lines 256-352):
- Path: `~/.config/fornax/config.toml`
- Loaded at app startup via `Config::load()` or `Config::try_load()`
- Saved via `Config::save()` with atomic write-and-rename
- Max file size: 1 MB
- Uses TOML format (serde_toml)

---

### 2. SETTINGS FLOW: Rust → Commands → TypeScript → UI

**Command Layer** (`/Users/heath/code/fornax/src/lib/tauri.ts`, lines 20-22):

```typescript
export const api = {
  getConfig: () => invoke<Config>("get_config"),
  saveConfig: (config: Config) => invoke<void>("save_config", { config }),
```

**TypeScript Config DTO** (`/Users/heath/code/fornax/src/types.ts`, lines 45-67):

```typescript
export interface Config {
  font_size: number;
  mono_font_size: number;
  ui_scale: number;
  dark_mode: boolean;
  theme: string;
  default_endpoint: string;
  system_prompt: string;
  temperature: number;
  max_tokens: number;
  use_max_tokens: boolean;
  saved_endpoints: Endpoint[];
  font_family: string;
  mono_font_family: string;
  top_p: number | null;
  frequency_penalty: number | null;
  presence_penalty: number | null;
  stop_sequences: string[];
  hotkeys: Hotkeys;
  mcp_servers?: McpServer[];
  usage_retention_days: number;
}
```

**UI Rendering** (`/Users/heath/code/fornax/src/components/SettingsModal.tsx`):

- Sections: "general", "endpoints", "generation", "appearance", "keyboard", "mcp"
- **General section** (lines 155-206): default endpoint, system prompt, usage retention
- **Endpoints section** (lines 209-318): endpoint URLs, API keys, runtime, prometheus_url, GPU source, agent_url
- **Generation section** (lines 320-426): temperature, max_tokens, use_max_tokens toggle, top_p, penalties, stop sequences
- **Appearance section** (lines 428-506): theme dropdown, UI scale, font sizes/families
- **Keyboard section** (lines 509-527): hotkey rebinding with capture UI
- **MCP section** (lines 529-666): MCP server management

**Settings Save Flow**:
1. User edits fields in SettingsModal → setState updates local `c` draft
2. Click "Save" button → calls `onSave(c)` callback
3. App.tsx `saveConfig` function → `api.saveConfig(config)` 
4. Invokes Tauri `save_config` command
5. Rust backend writes to `~/.config/fornax/config.toml` and reloads in-memory config

**Appearance Changes Apply Live** (without Save):
- `setAppearance` hook updates both state and calls `onApplyAppearance`
- Debounced 400ms save via `appearanceSaveTimer` in App.tsx

---

### 3. TOKEN COUNTING & CONTEXT WINDOW

**Token Counter** (`/Users/heath/code/fornax/src/lib/tokens.ts`):

```typescript
import { encode } from "gpt-tokenizer";

export function countTokens(text: string): number {
  try {
    return encode(text).length;
  } catch {
    return Math.ceil(text.length / 4);
  }
}
```

**Live Display** (`/Users/heath/code/fornax/src/components/ChatView.tsx`):
- Displays token count badge in the composer: `{props.tokenCount} tok`
- Only shown when count > 0

**Calculation** (`/Users/heath/code/fornax/src/App.tsx`):
```javascript
useEffect(() => {
  const t = setTimeout(() => {
    setTokenCount(composerInput.trim() ? countTokens(composerInput) : 0);
  }, 150);
  return () => clearTimeout(t);
}, [composerInput]);
```

**Current Implementation Issues**:
- Uses `gpt-tokenizer` (cl100k_base encoding) for live display only
- **No context-window size tracking anywhere** — no per-model metadata storage
- **No notion of available context length** — can't compute remaining tokens
- Authoritative token counts come from the API's `usage` event after generation
- No facility to query or store model context windows (e.g., Claude 3.5 = 200k, GPT-4 = 128k)

---

### 4. MESSAGE STORAGE & SCHEMA

**Message Table Schema** (`/Users/heath/code/fornax/src-tauri/src/core/storage.rs`, lines 151-158, 230-242):

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS messages (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    conversation_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    role TEXT NOT NULL,
    content TEXT NOT NULL,
    position INTEGER NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (conversation_id) REFERENCES conversations(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
);
```

**Version 2 Columns Added**:
```rust
const MSG_COLS: &[(&str, &str)] = &[
    ("parent_id", "INTEGER REFERENCES messages(id) ON DELETE CASCADE"),
    ("branch_index", "INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0"),
    ("created_at", "INTEGER"),
    ("tool_calls", "TEXT"),
    ("tool_call_id", "TEXT"),
];
```

**Message Rust Type** (`/Users/heath/code/fornax/src-tauri/src/core/message.rs`, lines 54-88):

```rust
#[derive(Clone, Debug, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct Message {
    pub role: Role,
    pub content: Vec<ContentPart>,
    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
    pub tool_calls: Option<Vec<ToolCall>>,
    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
    pub tool_call_id: Option<String>,
    #[serde(skip, default)]
    pub created_at: Option<i64>,
    #[serde(skip, default)]
    pub id: Option<i64>,
    #[serde(skip, default)]
    pub parent_id: Option<i64>,
    #[serde(skip, default)]
    pub branch_index: i64,
}
```

**ContentPart Enum** (`message.rs`, lines 40-52):

```rust
#[derive(Clone, Debug, Serialize, Deserialize, PartialEq)]
```

## Exploration Complete: Fornax Configuration, Token Counting, and Message Storage

I've mapped the complete end-to-end flow for configuration/settings, token counting, and message storage in the Fornax Tauri app. Here are the key findings:

---

### 1. CONFIGURATION & SETTINGS END-TO-END

**Rust Config Struct** (`/Users/heath/code/fornax/src-tauri/src/core/config.rs`, lines 188-226):

```rust
#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize, Clone, Debug)]
#[serde(default)]
pub struct Config {
    pub font_size: f32,
    pub mono_font_size: f32,
    pub ui_scale: f32,
    pub dark_mode: bool,
    pub theme: String,
    pub default_endpoint: String,
    pub system_prompt: String,
    pub temperature: f32,
    pub max_tokens: u32,
    pub use_max_tokens: bool,
    pub saved_endpoints: Vec<Endpoint>,
    pub font_family: String,
    pub mono_font_family: String,
    pub top_p: Option<f32>,
    pub frequency_penalty: Option<f32>,
    pub presence_penalty: Option<f32>,
    pub stop_sequences: Vec<String>,
    pub hotkeys: Hotkeys,
    #[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "Vec::is_empty")]
    pub mcp_servers: Vec<McpServer>,
    #[serde(default)]
    pub usage_retention_days: u32,
}
```

**Endpoint Struct** (`config.rs`, lines 53-128):

```rust
#[derive(Serialize, Clone, Debug)]
pub struct Endpoint {
    pub url: String,
    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
    pub api_key: Option<String>,
    #[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "runtime_is_default")]
    pub runtime: Runtime,
    #[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
    pub prometheus_url: Option<String>,
    #[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "gpu_is_default")]
    pub gpu: GpuKind,
    #[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
    pub agent_url: Option<String>,
}
```

**Theme Validation** (`config.rs`, lines 400-415):

```rust
const THEMES: &[&str] = &[
    "dark",
    "light",
    "catppuccin-latte",
    "catppuccin-frappe",
    "catppuccin-macchiato",
    "catppuccin-mocha",
];
```

**Config Sanitization** (`config.rs`, lines 354-415): The `Config::sanitize()` method validates:
- Font sizes: 8.0..=24.0
- UI scale: 0.75..=2.0
- Temperature: 0.0..=2.0
- Max tokens: 64..=16384
- Penalties: -2.0..=2.0 ranges
- Stop sequences: max 4, max 256 chars each

**Load/Save** (`config.rs`, lines 256-352):
- Path: `~/.config/fornax/config.toml`
- Loaded at app startup via `Config::load()` or `Config::try_load()`
- Saved via `Config::save()` with atomic write-and-rename
- Max file size: 1 MB
- Uses TOML format (serde_toml)

---

### 2. SETTINGS FLOW: Rust → Commands → TypeScript → UI

**Command Layer** (`/Users/heath/code/fornax/src/lib/tauri.ts`, lines 20-22):


**UI Rendering** (`/Users/heath/code/fornax/src/components/SettingsModal.tsx`):

- Sections: "general", "endpoints", "generation", "appearance", "keyboard", "mcp"
- **General section** (lines 155-206): default endpoint, system prompt, usage retention
- **Endpoints section** (lines 209-318): endpoint URLs, API keys, runtime, prometheus_url, GPU source, agent_url
- **Generation section** (lines 320-426): temperature, max_tokens, use_max_tokens toggle, top_p, penalties, stop sequences
- **Appearance section** (lines 428-506): theme dropdown, UI scale, font sizes/families
- **Keyboard section** (lines 509-527): hotkey rebinding with capture UI
- **MCP section** (lines 529-666): MCP server management

**Settings Save Flow**:
1. User edits fields in SettingsModal → setState updates local `c` draft
2. Click "Save" button → calls `onSave(c)` callback
3. App.tsx `saveConfig` function → `api.saveConfig(config)` 
4. Invokes Tauri `save_config` command
5. Rust backend writes to `~/.config/fornax/config.toml` and reloads in-memory config

**Appearance Changes Apply Live** (without Save):

- Debounced 400ms save via `appearanceSaveTimer` in App.tsx

---

### 3. TOKEN COUNTING & CONTEXT WINDOW

**Token Counter** (`/Users/heath/code/fornax/src/lib/tokens.ts`):

```typescript
import { encode } from "gpt-tokenizer";

export function countTokens(text: string): number {
  try {
    return encode(text).length;
  } catch {
    return Math.ceil(text.length / 4);
  }
}
```

**Live Display** (`/Users/heath/code/fornax/src/components/ChatView.tsx`):
- Displays token count badge in the composer: `{props.tokenCount} tok`
- Only shown when count > 0

**Calculation** (`/Users/heath/code/fornax/src/App.tsx`):
```javascript
useEffect(() => {
  const t = setTimeout(() => {
    setTokenCount(composerInput.trim() ? countTokens(composerInput) : 0);
  }, 150);
  return () => clearTimeout(t);
}, [composerInput]);
```

**Current Implementation Issues**:
- Uses `gpt-tokenizer` (cl100k_base encoding) for live display only
- **No context-window size tracking anywhere** — no per-model metadata storage
- **No notion of available context length** — can't compute remaining tokens
- Authoritative token counts come from the API's `usage` event after generation
- No facility to query or store model context windows (e.g., Claude 3.5 = 200k, GPT-4 = 128k)

Perfect. Now I have all the information I need. Let me create a comprehensive report:

## Fornax Tauri Chat Streaming & Turn Loop Architecture

### HIGH-LEVEL FLOW

**Chat lifecycle**: `send_message` → `run_turn` (orchestrates tool loop) → `stream_once` (one completion) → `request_approval` (user gates unsafe tools) → `execute_tool` (runs tools) → repeat until no tool calls or MAX_TOOL_ITERATIONS.

Messages persist to **SQLite** after each assistant turn and after tool execution. Conversation history is sent to the API inside `wire` (lines 1202–1209 in commands.rs).

---

### 1. COMMAND LAYER: `src-tauri/src/commands.rs`

#### Key Constants
- **`MAX_TOOL_ITERATIONS: 8`** (line 22) — max tool-call → re-stream cycles per user turn.

#### Core Functions

**`send_message` (lines 992–1050)**
- **Signature**: `pub async fn send_message(state: State<'_, AppState>, params: SendParams, on_event: Channel<ChatEvent>) -> Result<i64, String>`
- Creates conversation row if new (line 1001)
- **Appends user message to messages** (lines 1003–1015)
- **Saves messages to DB immediately** (line 1018): `storage.save_messages(conversation_id, &mut messages)?`
- Calls `run_turn` (line 1036) which returns `(final_id, errored)`
- Emits `ChatEvent::Done { message_id: final_id }` when stream ends (lines 1045–1047)

**`regenerate` (lines 1055–1096)**
- Finds the last user message in the conversation
- Truncates the message list to just that user message (line 1068): `let messages: Vec<Message> = full[..=idx].to_vec();`
- Calls `run_turn` with the truncated list (line 1085)

**`edit_message` (lines 1101–1148)**
- Finds the message to edit by id (line 1110)
- Creates a sibling (lines 1115–1121): new user message with `parent_id` set, `branch_index` = next branch under that parent
- Truncates history up to the original's parent (line 1117): `let mut messages: Vec<Message> = full[..idx].to_vec();`
- Calls `run_turn` with the new list (line 1138)

**`run_turn` (lines 1155–1368)**
- **Core turn orchestration function**
- **Registers CancellationToken** upfront (lines 1167–1172):
  ```rust
  let cancel = CancellationToken::new();
  state.cancel.lock().unwrap().insert(conversation_id, cancel.clone());
  ```
  This allows a concurrent `cancel_stream` call to stop the stream mid-flight.
- **Loads tools** fresh each turn (line 1176): `tools::load_from_dir(&tools::user_tools_dir())`
- **Loop** (line 1196): `for iteration in 0..MAX_TOOL_ITERATIONS`
  - **Checks cancellation** (lines 1197–1199): `if cancel.is_cancelled() { break; }`
  - **Emits `TurnStart`** (line 1200)
  - **Assembles wire** (lines 1202–1209):
    ```rust
    let mut wire = Vec::with_capacity(messages.len() + 2);
    if !gp.system_prompt.trim().is_empty() {
        wire.push(Message::text(Role::System, gp.system_prompt.clone()));
    }
    if let Some(sp) = &skills_prompt { wire.push(...); }
    wire.extend(messages.iter().cloned());
    ```
    **This is where context compaction would hook in**: trim/collapse old messages before pushing to API.
  - **Calls `stream_once`** (line 1212): `let outcome = stream_once(gp, wire, api_key.clone(), tools_api.clone(), cancel.clone(), on_event).await;`
  - **Records usage** (lines 1218–1228)
  - **Persists assistant message** (lines 1237–1247): Creates `Message` with content + reasoning + tool_calls, **saves to DB** via `storage.save_messages(...)`
  - **Executes tools** in a tool loop (lines 1259–1355):
    - Sends `ToolCall` event to UI
    - Handles `use_skill` (in-process) — returns skill body from memory
    - Handles MCP tools — routed to MCP manager with approval gate
    - Handles local tools — runs via `execute_tool` (line 1341)
    - Sends `ToolResult` event to UI
    - **Appends tool result message** (line 1354): `messages.push(Message::tool_result(call.id.clone(), result));`
    - **Saves again** (line 1357): `storage.save_messages(conversation_id, &mut messages)?`
  - **Loop continues if tool calls exist and iteration < MAX_TOOL_ITERATIONS** (line 1255)
- **Cleans up CancellationToken** (line 1366): `state.cancel.lock().unwrap().remove(&conversation_id);`
- **Returns** (line 1367): `(final_message_id, errored)`

**`stream_once` (lines 1435–1548)**
- **Signature**: `async fn stream_once(gp: &GenParams, wire: Vec<Message>, api_key: Option<String>, tools: Option<Vec<serde_json::Value>>, cancel: CancellationToken, on_event: &Channel<ChatEvent>) -> StreamOutcome`
- **Builds ChatParams** (lines 1444–1464) from GenParams + the wire messages
- **Spawns the streaming task** (line 1465): `api::stream_chat(chat_params, tx, cancel);`
- **Consumes stream events** in a loop (lines 1477–1512):
  - `Token(t)` → accumulate in `content`, emit to UI
  - `Reasoning(r)` → accumulate separately, emit to UI
  - `ToolCalls(calls)` → store for assembly
  - `UsageInfo(u)` → emit to UI, store for DB
  - `Error(msg)` → set `errored = true`, emit to UI
  - `Done` → break
- **Computes latency metrics** (lines 1514–1527): TTFT, decode throughput, prefill throughput
- **Returns `StreamOutcome`** (lines 1537–1547) with content, reasoning, tool_calls, usage, and metrics

**`cancel_stream` (lines 904–908)**
- **Signature**: `pub fn cancel_stream(state: State<'_, AppState>, conversation_id: i64)`
- **Looks up conversation's CancellationToken** in `state.cancel` (line 905)
- **Calls `.cancel()` on it** (line 906), which wakes the `tokio::select!` in `api::stream_chat` (line 376)

**`resolve_tool` (lines 914–931)**
- **Signature**: `pub fn resolve_tool(state: State<'_, AppState>, call_id: String, decision: String)`
- **Looks up the parked tool approval** by call_id (line 915)
- **Sends the decision** through the oneshot (line 929): `p.tx.send(approved)`
- Stores auto-approved tools in `state.auto_approved` if decision is "approve_all" (lines 920–924)

---

### 2. STATE LAYER: `src-tauri/src/state.rs`

**`AppState` struct (lines 24–42)**
```rust
pub struct AppState {
    pub storage: Mutex<Storage>,
    pub config: Mutex<Config>,
    /// Cancellation tokens keyed by conversation_id
    pub cancel: Mutex<HashMap<i64, CancellationToken>>,
    /// Tool calls awaiting approval, keyed by tool_call id
    pub pending_approvals: Mutex<HashMap<String, PendingApproval>>,
    /// (conversation_id, tool_name) pairs auto-approved for the session
    pub auto_approved: Mutex<HashSet<(i64, String)>>,
    pub mcp: Arc<McpManager>,
}
```

**`PendingApproval` struct (lines 12–16)**

```rust
    pub tx: oneshot::Sender<bool>,
    pub conversation_id: i64,
    pub tool: String,
}
```

**`api_key_for` method (lines 64–70)**
- Resolves API key for an endpoint from `config.saved_endpoints`
- **Returns `None` for local servers** (no auth needed)

---

### 3. STREAMING API: `src-tauri/src/core/api.rs`

#### StreamEvent Enum (lines 136–159)
```rust
pub enum StreamEvent {
    Token(String),                  // content token
    Reasoning(String),              // separate reasoning field (DeepSeek, etc.)
    ToolCalls(Vec<ToolCall>),       // assembled tool calls at end-of-stream
    Done,
    Error(String),
    UsageInfo(Usage),               // token counts + cost from API
    ModelsLoaded { url: String, models: Vec<String> },
}
```

**`Usage` struct (lines 89–95)**
```rust
pub struct Usage {
    pub prompt_tokens: Option<u32>,
    pub completion_tokens: Option<u32>,
    pub total_tokens: Option<u32>,
    pub cost: Option<f64>,          // OpenRouter-specific
}
```

**`stream_chat` (lines 266–480)**
- **Signature**: `pub fn stream_chat(params: ChatParams, tx: mpsc::UnboundedSender<StreamEvent>, cancel: CancellationToken)`
- **Spawns a tokio task** (line 271) that runs async
- **Builds HTTP request** (lines 305–319):
  - Includes `stream_options: { include_usage: true }` (line 316–318) to request usage chunks
  - Messages are serialized directly from `ChatParams.messages` (line 307)
- **Sends POST to `/chat/completions`** (line 331)
- **Streams bytes** (line 363): `let mut stream = resp.bytes_stream();`
- **Main loop** (lines 374–478): `tokio::select!`
  - **Branch 1**: `cancel.cancelled()` (lines 376–379) — stops the loop, emits `Done`
  - **Branch 2**: Next chunk arrives (lines 381–477)
    - Buffers bytes, handles UTF-8 boundaries (lines 401–414)
    - Parses SSE lines (lines 417–435)
    - **For each `data:` line**, deserializes a `ChatChunk` (line 437)
    - **Accumulates tool call deltas** (lines 459–463): per-slot accumulator
    - **On `[DONE]`** (line 431): flushes tool calls, emits `Done`, returns
    - **On `usage` chunk** (line 467): emits `UsageInfo`
    - **On `delta.content`** (lines 454–458): emits `Token` (skips empty strings)
    - **On `delta.reasoning`** (lines 449–453): emits `Reasoning`

**`accumulate_tool_call` (lines 486–505)**
- Merges per-chunk tool-call deltas into a slot-based accumulator
- `id` and `name` are set once; `arguments` are concatenated piecewise

**`flush_tool_calls` (lines 511–542)**
- Drains the accumulator into a final `ToolCalls` event
- Drops slots missing an id (diagnostic log)

---

### 4. MESSAGE STRUCTURE: `src-tauri/src/core/message.rs`

**`Message` struct (lines 54–88)**
```rust
pub struct Message {
    pub role: Role,
    pub content: Vec<ContentPart>,  // Vec of Text or ImageUrl parts
    pub tool_calls: Option<Vec<ToolCall>>,
    pub tool_call_id: Option<String>,
    pub created_at: Option<i64>,    // Unix epoch ms (session-local for live)
    pub id: Option<i64>,            // SQLite rowid; None until persisted
    pub parent_id: Option<i64>,     // For branching tree
    pub branch_index: i64,          // Sibling index under parent
}
```

**`Role` enum (lines 3–12)**
```rust
pub enum Role {
    System,
    User,
    Assistant,
    Tool,  // Tool result message
}
```

**`ContentPart` enum (lines 40–45)**
```rust
pub enum ContentPart {
    Text { text: String },
    ImageUrl { image_url: ImageUrl },
}
```

---

### 5. PERSISTENCE LAYER: `src-tauri/src/core/storage.rs`

**`save_messages` (lines 615–720)**
- **Signature**: `pub fn save_messages(&self, conversation_id: i64, messages: &mut [Message]) -> Result<(), String>`
- **Transaction-based** (lines 620–623)
- **For each message**:
  - **If `msg.id` is `Some(id)`** (lines 655–675): UPDATE existing (mid-stream appends):
    ```rust
    UPDATE messages SET content = ?1, created_at = ?2,
                        tool_calls = ?3, tool_call_id = ?4
     WHERE id = ?5
    ```
  - **If `msg.id` is `None`** (lines 677–720): INSERT new:
    ```rust
    INSERT INTO messages
     (conversation_id, role, content, position, parent_id, branch_index,
      created_at, tool_calls, tool_call_id)
     VALUES (...)
    ```
    - Auto-links `parent_id` if not set (lines 682–692)
    - Captures new rowid back into `msg.id` (line 713)

**`load_messages` (lines 764+)**
- Loads all messages for a conversation from DB, ordered by `position` (insertion order)
- Reconstructs the active path for display (newest branch at each fork)

---

### 6. FRONTEND: `src/App.tsx` & `src/components/ChatView.tsx`

#### App.tsx

**Per-conversation streaming runtime** (lines 131–145):
```typescript
interface ConvRuntime {
  messages: ChatMessage[];
  streaming: boolean;
}

interface StreamSession {
  convId: number;
  reasoning: string;
  content: string;
}
```

**`runStream` (lines 771–835)**
- **Signature**: `const runStream = async (startId: number, starter: (h: (ev: ChatEvent) => void) => Promise<number>)`
- **Takes a starter function** (e.g., `api.sendMessage`) that returns the conversation id once stream finishes
- **Creates a `StreamSession`** (lines 781–785) to bind events to that stream's conversation
- **Sets `streaming: true`** (line 788)
- **Calls `starter` with the event handler** (line 797): `await starter((ev) => handleEvent(ev, session));`
- **Finally block** (lines 800–834):
  - Sets `streaming: false`
  - Clears pending approvals
  - **Reloads conversation from DB** (line 818): `const msgs = await reloadConv(fid);`
  - Auto-opens newly generated artifacts if the chat is visible (lines 822–830)

**`handleEvent` (lines 606–742)**
- **Shared handler** for all streaming events from send/regenerate/edit
- **Case `started`** (lines 608–634): Migrates temp id to real id, updates sidebar
- **Case `turn_start`** (lines 636–651): Closes previous assistant turn, opens fresh streaming placeholder
- **Case `token`** (lines 653–658): Accumulates content, patches last assistant message
- **Case `reasoning`** (lines 660–665): Accumulates reasoning, patches last assistant message
- **Case `tool_call`** (lines 667–674): Appends tool call to `toolCalls` array on last assistant message
- **Case `tool_approval`** (lines 676–684): Stores pending approval by conversation
- **Case `tool_result`** (lines 686–697): Appends tool result message to chat
- **Case `usage`** (lines 699–706): Updates metrics display
- **Case `request_metrics`** (lines 708–724): Updates TTFT/decode/throughput charts
- **Case `error`** (lines 726–727): Shows error toast
- **Case `done`** (lines 729–739): Marks streaming false, assigns persisted id to last assistant message

**`send` (lines 837–858)**
- Allocates a temp id for new chats (line 841): `const startId = activeConvId ?? nextTempId.current--;`
- Appends user message optimistically (lines 843–846)
- **Calls `runStream` with `api.sendMessage`** (lines 847–857)

**`regenerate` (lines 860–875)**
- Drops trailing assistant/tool messages (lines 864–870)
- **Calls `runStream` with `api.regenerate`**

**`editMessage` (lines 877–896)**
- Truncates to edited message's prefix, appends new user text (lines 880–884)
- **Calls `runStream` with `api.editMessage`**

---

#### ChatView.tsx

**`send` button logic (lines 187–201)**
```typescript
const send = () => {
  if (props.streaming) return;  // ← **Disabled during streaming**
  if (input.trim().startsWith("/") && props.onSlash(input)) {
    onInputChange("");
    resetHeight();
    return;
  }
  const t = input.trim();
  if (!t && attachments.length === 0) return;
  props.onSend(t, attachments);  // ← Calls App.send()
  onInputChange("");
  setAttachments([]);
  resetHeight();
};
```

**Composer button state** (lines 464–479)
```typescript
{props.streaming ? (
  <button className="stop-btn" onClick={props.onStop}>
    ■ Stop
  </button>
) : (
  <button className="send-btn" onClick={send}
    disabled={(!input.trim() && attachments.length === 0) || !props.settings.model}>
    Send
  </button>
)}
```
- **Send button is disabled during streaming** (line 472)
- **Stop button replaces it** (line 465)
- User cannot send while a stream is in flight; they must click Stop first

---

### 7. TYPED WRAPPERS: `src/lib/tauri.ts` & `src/types.ts`

**`api.sendMessage` (lines 66–70)**
```typescript
sendMessage: (params: SendParams, onEvent: (ev: ChatEvent) => void) => {
  const channel = new Channel<ChatEvent>();
  channel.onmessage = onEvent;
  return invoke<number>("send_message", { params, onEvent: channel });
}
```
- Creates a Tauri `Channel` for streaming events
- Returns a Promise that resolves to the conversation id when the stream finishes

**`ChatEvent` type** (types.ts lines 277–306)
- Tagged union of all possible streaming event types
- Deserialized from Rust `ChatEvent` enum

---

## IMPLEMENTATION ROADMAP

### (A) AUTO-COMPACTION OF CONVERSATION HISTORY

**Where to hook in**: `/Users/heath/code/fornax/src-tauri/src/commands.rs`, lines 1202–1209 in `run_turn`, **before `stream_once` is called**.

**Current code**:
```rust
let mut wire = Vec::with_capacity(messages.len() + 2);
if !gp.system_prompt.trim().is_empty() {
    wire.push(Message::text(Role::System, gp.system_prompt.clone()));
}
if let Some(sp) = &skills_prompt {
    wire.push(Message::text(Role::System, sp.clone()));
}
wire.extend(messages.iter().cloned());

let outcome = stream_once(gp, wire, api_key.clone(), tools_api.clone(), cancel.clone(), on_event).await;
```

**Hook point**: After building `wire` but before `stream_once`, measure token count of the wire and apply a compaction strategy:
1. **Tokenize the full `wire`** using an embedded tokenizer (e.g., `tiktoken-rs`)
2. **If tokens exceed a threshold** (e.g., 60k), apply compaction:
   - Keep the system prompt (always)
   - Keep the last N user/assistant exchanges (e.g., last 5 turns)
   - **Collapse middle messages** into a summary or drop them entirely
3. **Emit a `CompactionApplied` event** to the UI (optional, for transparency)
4. **Pass the compacted `wire` to `stream_once`**

**Key files to modify**:
- `src-tauri/src/commands.rs` — add compaction logic in `run_turn` (lines 1202–1209)
- `src-tauri/src/core/api.rs` — no changes; just the wire passed to it is smaller
- Storage is **not** affected; only the in-memory wire is trimmed (conversation history stays on disk for reload/branch navigation)

**Usage info is not lost** because it comes back in `StreamEvent::UsageInfo` and is persisted via `storage.record_usage()` on line 1219.

---

### (B) "STEERING" (INJECTING/QUEUING USER INPUT WHILE STREAMING)

**Challenge**: The send button is **disabled during streaming** (ChatView.tsx line 188). To enable mid-stream injection, you must:

1. **Allow the composer to accept input while streaming** (ChatView.tsx line 188)
2. **Queue the injected message** in the frontend state
3. **After the current turn finishes**, automatically call `send()` with the queued message

**Implementation outline**:

**Frontend (App.tsx)**:
```typescript
const [queuedUserInput, setQueuedUserInput] = useState<string | null>(null);

const sendWhileStreaming = (text: string) => {
  if (streaming) {
    setQueuedUserInput(text);  // Queue it
    return;
  }
  send(text);  // Send immediately
};

// In runStream's finally block (after reloadConv):
if (queuedUserInput) {
  const temp = queuedUserInput;
  setQueuedUserInput(null);
  send(temp);  // Auto-send queued message
}
```

**Backend (commands.rs)**:
- **No changes needed** — the turn loop already handles queued messages naturally (each `send_message` creates a fresh turn)

**UI Changes (ChatView.tsx)**:
```typescript
const send = () => {
  if (props.streaming) {
    // Instead of returning, queue and signal UI
    setQueuedInput(input);
    setShowQueuedIndicator(true);
    onInputChange("");  // Clear composer but remember intent
    return;
  }
  // ... existing send logic
};
```

**Key insight**: The architecture already supports **concurrent streams in different conversations** (line 131–145, `ConvRuntime` per conversation). Steering is just about queueing within the active conversation and auto-triggering after the stream ends.

---

## CANCELLATION & STOPPING

**Exact mechanism** (commands.rs lines 904–908, api.rs lines 376–379):
1. User clicks Stop button → calls `App.stop()` (App.tsx line 913)
2. `App.stop()` calls `api.cancelStream(activeConvId)` (App.tsx line 915)
3. `api.cancelStream` fires the Tauri command `cancel_stream` (tauri.ts line 57)
4. Backend `cancel_stream` (commands.rs line 904–908) **looks up the conversation's `CancellationToken`** and calls `.cancel()` on it
5. The token is **registered at the top of `run_turn`** (lines 1167–1172) so it's available before any tool/MCP discovery
6. **Inside `stream_chat`** (api.rs lines 375–380), the main loop **uses `tokio::select!`** with `cancel.cancelled()` as a branch
7. When cancelled, it **flushes any pending tool calls, emits `Done`, and returns** (lines 377–379)
8. **`stream_once` consumes the `Done` event** (api.rs line 1509) and breaks its loop
9. **`run_turn` detects cancellation** (lines 1197–1199) and breaks the tool loop
10. The turn finishes, frontend `runStream` finally block runs (lines 800–834), sets `streaming: false`

**Message persistence during cancellation**:
- If a stream is cancelled **mid-turn** (tokens have arrived but we're not yet at tool-call evaluation), the partial assistant message was already saved to the DB (lines 1245–1247 on the *previous* iteration, or no message has been saved yet if this is iteration 0)
- The next tool iteration or turn restart will not see the partial message unless it was already persisted
- Currently, **the partial message is lost** (discarded in the finally block on line 805)

**To preserve partial cancellations**: After stopping, save the session's `content` + `reasoning` to the assistant message and persist it, or emit it as a final "partial result" event.

---

## SUMMARY TABLE

| **Component** | **File** | **Key Function** | **Line Range** | **Purpose** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Command orchestration** | `commands.rs` | `run_turn` | 1155–1368 | Tool loop, messaging, persists after each turn |
| | | `stream_once` | 1435–1548 | One streaming completion, emits events |
| | | `send_message` | 992–1050 | Entry point for user send |
| | | `cancel_stream` | 904–908 | Stops active stream via CancellationToken |
| | | `resolve_tool` | 914–931 | Gates unsafe tool calls |
| **State** | `state.rs` | `AppState` | 24–42 | Holds CancellationTokens, pending approvals, auto-approved tools |
| **Streaming API** | `api.rs` | `stream_chat` | 266–480 | HTTP SSE streaming loop, tool accumulation, cancellation handling |
| | | `StreamEvent` | 136–159 | Enum of all event types (Token, ToolCall, Usage, Done, Error) |
| **Messages** | `message.rs` | `Message` struct | 54–88 | Role, content, tool_calls, id, parent_id, branch_index |
| **Persistence** | `storage.rs` | `save_messages` | 615–720 | INSERT/UPDATE messages in SQLite transaction |
| **Frontend orchestration** | `App.tsx` | `runStream` | 771–835 | Wraps streaming, manages session, reloads DB, auto-opens artifacts |
| | | `handleEvent` | 606–742 | Dispatches ChatEvent to state updates |
| | | `send`, `regenerate`, `editMessage` | 837–896 | Call runStream with different starters |
| **UI** | `ChatView.tsx` | `send` button | 187–201 | **Disabled during streaming** (line 188) |
| | | Button state | 464–479 | Stop button replaces Send during streaming |
| **Type defs** | `types.ts` | `ChatEvent` | 277–306 | Tagged union of all event types |
| | `tauri.ts` | `api.sendMessage` | 66–70 | Creates Channel, invokes Tauri command |

---

## WHERE TO IMPLEMENT EACH FEATURE

### (A) Auto-Compaction
- **Primary**: `/Users/heath/code/fornax/src-tauri/src/commands.rs` lines 1202–1209 (in `run_turn`, before `stream_once`)
- **Secondary**: Add optional new `ChatEvent::CompactionApplied { old_tokens, new_tokens, dropped_messages }` in types/api for UI feedback
- **No database changes**: Compaction is in-memory; history stays on disk

### (B) Steering
- **Frontend**: `/Users/heath/code/fornax/src/App.tsx` lines 771–835 (in `runStream` finally block) + add queued input state
- **UI**: `/Users/heath/code/fornax/src/components/ChatView.tsx` lines 187–201 (allow send while streaming, show queue indicator)
- **Backend**: No changes; turns are naturally queued by the command layer

Both features **leverage the existing CancellationToken, message persistence, and turn loop** without structural changes.

content.text:
Perfect. Now I have all the information I need. Let me create a comprehensive report:

## Fornax Tauri Chat Streaming & Turn Loop Architecture

### HIGH-LEVEL FLOW

**Chat lifecycle**: `send_message` → `run_turn` (orchestrates tool loop) → `stream_once` (one completion) → `request_approval` (user gates unsafe tools) → `execute_tool` (runs tools) → repeat until no tool calls or MAX_TOOL_ITERATIONS.

Messages persist to **SQLite** after each assistant turn and after tool execution. Conversation history is sent to the API inside `wire` (lines 1202–1209 in commands.rs).

---

### 1. COMMAND LAYER: `src-tauri/src/commands.rs`

#### Key Constants
- **`MAX_TOOL_ITERATIONS: 8`** (line 22) — max tool-call → re-stream cycles per user turn.

#### Core Functions

**`send_message` (lines 992–1050)**
- **Signature**: `pub async fn send_message(state: State<'_, AppState>, params: SendParams, on_event: Channel<ChatEvent>) -> Result<i64, String>`
- Creates conversation row if new (line 1001)
- **Appends user message to messages** (lines 1003–1015)
- **Saves messages to DB immediately** (line 1018): `storage.save_messages(conversation_id, &mut messages)?`
- Calls `run_turn` (line 1036) which returns `(final_id, errored)`
- Emits `ChatEvent::Done { message_id: final_id }` when stream ends (lines 1045–1047)

**`regenerate` (lines 1055–1096)**
- Finds the last user message in the conversation
- Truncates the message list to just that user message (line 1068): `let messages: Vec<Message> = full[..=idx].to_vec();`
- Calls `run_turn` with the truncated list (line 1085)

**`edit_message` (lines 1101–1148)**
- Finds the message to edit by id (line 1110)
- Creates a sibling (lines 1115–1121): new user message with `parent_id` set, `branch_index` = next branch under that parent
- Truncates history up to the original's parent (line 1117): `let mut messages: Vec<Message> = full[..idx].to_vec();`
- Calls `run_turn` with the new list (line 1138)

**`run_turn` (lines 1155–1368)**
- **Core turn orchestration function**
- **Registers CancellationToken** upfront (lines 1167–1172):
  ```rust
  let cancel = CancellationToken::new();
  state.cancel.lock().unwrap().insert(conversation_id, cancel.clone());
  ```
  This allows a concurrent `cancel_stream` call to stop the stream mid-flight.
- **Loads tools** fresh each turn (line 1176): `tools::load_from_dir(&tools::user_tools_dir())`
- **Loop** (line 1196): `for iteration in 0..MAX_TOOL_ITERATIONS`
  - **Checks cancellation** (lines 1197–1199): `if cancel.is_cancelled() { break; }`
  - **Emits `TurnStart`** (line 1200)
  - **Assembles wire** (lines 1202–1209):
    ```rust
    let mut wire = Vec::with_capacity(messages.len() + 2);
    if !gp.system_prompt.trim().is_empty() {
        wire.push(Message::text(Role::System, gp.system_prompt.clone()));
    }
    if let Some(sp) = &skills_prompt { wire.push(...); }
    wire.extend(messages.iter().cloned());
    ```
    **This is where context compaction would hook in**: trim/collapse old messages before pushing to API.
  - **Calls `stream_once`** (line 1212): `let outcome = stream_once(gp, wire, api_key.clone(), tools_api.clone(), cancel.clone(), on_event).await;`
  - **Records usage** (lines 1218–1228)
  - **Persists assistant message** (lines 1237–1247): Creates `Message` with content + reasoning + tool_calls, **saves to DB** via `storage.save_messages(...)`
  - **Executes tools** in a tool loop (lines 1259–1355):
    - Sends `ToolCall` event to UI
    - Handles `use_skill` (in-process) — returns skill body from memory
    - Handles MCP tools — routed to MCP manager with approval gate
    - Handles local tools — runs via `execute_tool` (line 1341)
    - Sends `ToolResult` event to UI
    - **Appends tool result message** (line 1354): `messages.push(Message::tool_result(call.id.clone(), result));`
    - **Saves again** (line 1357): `storage.save_messages(conversation_id, &mut messages)?`
  - **Loop continues if tool calls exist and iteration < MAX_TOOL_ITERATIONS** (line 1255)

**`stream_once` (lines 1435–1548)**
- **Signature**: `async fn stream_once(gp: &GenParams, wire: Vec<Message>, api_key: Option<String>, tools: Option<Vec<serde_json::Value>>, cancel: CancellationToken, on_event: &Channel<ChatEvent>) -> StreamOutcome`
- **Builds ChatParams** (lines 1444–1464) from GenParams + the wire messages

  - `Reasoning(r)` → accumulate separately, emit to UI

  - `UsageInfo(u)` → emit to UI, store for DB

  - `Done` → break
- **Computes latency metrics** (lines 1514–1527): TTFT, decode throughput, prefill throughput

## IMPLEMENTATION ROADMAP

### (A) AUTO-COMPACTION OF CONVERSATION HISTORY

**Where to hook in**: `/Users/heath/code/fornax/src-tauri/src/commands.rs`, lines 1202–1209 in `run_turn`, **before `stream_once` is called**.

**Current code**:
```rust
let mut wire = Vec::with_capacity(messages.len() + 2);
if !gp.system_prompt.trim().is_empty() {
    wire.push(Message::text(Role::System, gp.system_prompt.clone()));
}
if let Some(sp) = &skills_prompt {
    wire.push(Message::text(Role::System, sp.clone()));
}
wire.extend(messages.iter().cloned());

let outcome = stream_once(gp, wire, api_key.clone(), tools_api.clone(), cancel.clone(), on_event).await;
```

**Hook point**: After building `wire` but before `stream_once`, measure token count of the wire and apply a compaction strategy:
1. **Tokenize the full `wire`** using an embedded tokenizer (e.g., `tiktoken-rs`)
2. **If tokens exceed a threshold** (e.g., 60k), apply compaction:
   - Keep the system prompt (always)
   - Keep the last N user/assistant exchanges (e.g., last 5 turns)
   - **Collapse middle messages** into a summary or drop them entirely
3. **Emit a `CompactionApplied` event** to the UI (optional, for transparency)
4. **Pass the compacted `wire` to `stream_once`**

**Key files to modify**:
- `src-tauri/src/commands.rs` — add compaction logic in `run_turn` (lines 1202–1209)
- `src-tauri/src/core/api.rs` — no changes; just the wire passed to it is smaller
- Storage is **not** affected; only the in-memory wire is trimmed (conversation history stays on disk for reload/branch navigation)

**Usage info is not lost** because it comes back in `StreamEvent::UsageInfo` and is persisted via `storage.record_usage()` on line 1219.

---

### (B) "STEERING" (INJECTING/QUEUING USER INPUT WHILE STREAMING)

**Challenge**: The send button is **disabled during streaming** (ChatView.tsx line 188). To enable mid-stream injection, you must:

1. **Allow the composer to accept input while streaming** (ChatView.tsx line 188)
2. **Queue the injected message** in the frontend state
3. **After the current turn finishes**, automatically call `send()` with the queued message

**Implementation outline**:

**Frontend (App.tsx)**:
```typescript
const [queuedUserInput, setQueuedUserInput] = useState<string | null>(null);

const sendWhileStreaming = (text: string) => {
  if (streaming) {
    setQueuedUserInput(text);  // Queue it
    return;
  }
  send(text);  // Send immediately
};

// In runStream's finally block (after reloadConv):
if (queuedUserInput) {
  const temp = queuedUserInput;
  setQueuedUserInput(null);
  send(temp);  // Auto-send queued message
}
```

**Backend (commands.rs)**:
- **No changes needed** — the turn loop already handles queued messages naturally (each `send_message` creates a fresh turn)

**UI Changes (ChatView.tsx)**:
```typescript
const send = () => {
  if (props.streaming) {
    // Instead of returning, queue and signal UI
    setQueuedInput(input);
    setShowQueuedIndicator(true);
    onInputChange("");  // Clear composer but remember intent
    return;
  }
  // ... existing send logic
};
```

**Key insight**: The architecture already supports **concurrent streams in different conversations** (line 131–145, `ConvRuntime` per conversation). Steering is just about queueing within the active conversation and auto-triggering after the stream ends.

---

## CANCELLATION & STOPPING

**Exact mechanism** (commands.rs lines 904–908, api.rs lines 376–379):
1. User clicks Stop button → calls `App.stop()` (App.tsx line 913)
2. `App.stop()` calls `api.cancelStream(activeConvId)` (App.tsx line 915)
3. `api.cancelStream` fires the Tauri command `cancel_stream` (tauri.ts line 57)
4. Backend `cancel_stream` (commands.rs line 904–908) **looks up the conversation's `CancellationToken`** and calls `.cancel()` on it
5. The token is **registered at the top of `run_turn`** (lines 1167–1172) so it's available before any tool/MCP discovery
6. **Inside `stream_chat`** (api.rs lines 375–380), the main loop **uses `tokio::select!`** with `cancel.cancelled()` as a branch
7. When cancelled, it **flushes any pending tool calls, emits `Done`, and returns** (lines 377–379)
8. **`stream_once` consumes the `Done` event** (api.rs line 1509) and breaks its loop
9. **`run_turn` detects cancellation** (lines 1197–1199) and breaks the tool loop
10. The turn finishes, frontend `runStream` finally block runs (lines 800–834), sets `streaming: false`

**Message persistence during cancellation**:
- If a stream is cancelled **mid-turn** (tokens have arrived but we're not yet at tool-call evaluation), the partial assistant message was already saved to the DB (lines 1245–1247 on the *previous* iteration, or no message has been saved yet if this is iteration 0)
- The next tool iteration or turn restart will not see the partial message unless it was already persisted
- Currently, **the partial message is lost** (discarded in the finally block on line 805)

**To preserve partial cancellations**: After stopping, save the session's `content` + `reasoning` to the assistant message and persist it, or emit it as a final "partial result" event.

---

## SUMMARY TABLE

| **Component** | **File** | **Key Function** | **Line Range** | **Purpose** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Command orchestration** | `commands.rs` | `run_turn` | 1155–1368 | Tool loop, messaging, persists after each turn |
| | | `stream_once` | 1435–1548 | One streaming completion, emits events |
| | | `send_message` | 992–1050 | Entry point for user send |
| | | `cancel_stream` | 904–908 | Stops active stream via CancellationToken |
| | | `resolve_tool` | 914–931 | Gates unsafe tool calls |
| **State** | `state.rs` | `AppState` | 24–42 | Holds CancellationTokens, pending approvals, auto-approved tools |
| **Streaming API** | `api.rs` | `stream_chat` | 266–480 | HTTP SSE streaming loop, tool accumulation, cancellation handling |
| | | `StreamEvent` | 136–159 | Enum of all event types (Token, ToolCall, Usage, Done, Error) |
| **Messages** | `message.rs` | `Message` struct | 54–88 | Role, content, tool_calls, id, parent_id, branch_index |
| **Persistence** | `storage.rs` | `save_messages` | 615–720 | INSERT/UPDATE messages in SQLite transaction |
| **Frontend orchestration** | `App.tsx` | `runStream` | 771–835 | Wraps streaming, manages session, reloads DB, auto-opens artifacts |
| | | `handleEvent` | 606–742 | Dispatches ChatEvent to state updates |
| | | `send`, `regenerate`, `editMessage` | 837–896 | Call runStream with different starters |
| **UI** | `ChatView.tsx` | `send` button | 187–201 | **Disabled during streaming** (line 188) |
| | | Button state | 464–479 | Stop button replaces Send during streaming |
| **Type defs** | `types.ts` | `ChatEvent` | 277–306 | Tagged union of all event types |
| | `tauri.ts` | `api.sendMessage` | 66–70 | Creates Channel, invokes Tauri command |

---

## WHERE TO IMPLEMENT EACH FEATURE

### (A) Auto-Compaction
- **Primary**: `/Users/heath/code/fornax/src-tauri/src/commands.rs` lines 1202–1209 (in `run_turn`, before `stream_once`)
- **Secondary**: Add optional new `ChatEvent::CompactionApplied { old_tokens, new_tokens, dropped_messages }` in types/api for UI feedback
- **No database changes**: Compaction is in-memory; history stays on disk

### (B) Steering
- **Frontend**: `/Users/heath/code/fornax/src/App.tsx` lines 771–835 (in `runStream` finally block) + add queued input state
- **UI**: `/Users/heath/code/fornax/src/components/ChatView.tsx` lines 187–201 (allow send while streaming, show queue indicator)
- **Backend**: No changes; turns are naturally queued by the command layer

Both features **leverage the existing CancellationToken, message persistence, and turn loop** without structural changes.

content.text: I have enough architectural understanding from the exploration. The two features have genuine design forks that change the implementation. Let me clarify those.

questions.question: What should "steering" do when you send a message while the assistant is streaming?

options.label: Interrupt & redirect
options.description: Sending mid-stream cancels the current generation immediately, keeps the partial output, appends your new message, and continues. Best for course-correcting a response going the wrong way.

options.label: Queue for next turn
options.description: Let the current turn finish, then auto-send your queued message as the next turn. Non-disruptive; like typing ahead in Claude Code.

options.label: Both (queue + interrupt key)
options.description: Default to queueing; a modifier (e.g. Shift+Enter or a separate button) interrupts immediately. More UI but most flexible.

questions.question: How should auto-compaction shrink the context when it triggers?

options.label: LLM summary (like Claude Code)
options.description: Make a side LLM call to summarize older messages into a compact summary, then continue with summary + recent messages. Highest quality, costs one extra call, needs a summary prompt.

options.label: Sliding-window truncation
options.description: Drop oldest messages (keeping system prompt + most recent N turns) with no summary. Free and instant, but loses older context entirely.

options.label: Truncate now, summary later
options.description: Ship truncation first as the simpler path, structure the code so an LLM-summary mode can be added later.

questions.question: Compaction triggers at a token threshold, but the app currently tracks no per-model context-window size. How should the threshold be set?

options.label: Per-endpoint context window + %
options.description: Add a configurable context-window size per endpoint (e.g. 128k) and compact at a % of it (e.g. 80%). More accurate per-backend, slightly more config.

options.label: Single absolute token threshold
options.description: One global 'compact at N tokens' setting in config. Simplest to build and reason about; you tune it to your smallest model.

questions.question: Should compaction settings be global-only or also per-conversation?

options.label: Global only (for now)
options.description: Live in the main Config / SettingsModal. Simplest; matches where most generation defaults live.

options.label: Global + per-conversation override
options.description: Add overrides to ConversationSettings too, like temperature/model. More plumbing but consistent with existing per-chat overrides.

How should the threshold be set?"="Per-endpoint context window + %", "Should compaction settings be global-only or also per-conversation?"="Global + per-conversation override".
